article.title: EU Delays High-Risk AI Act Rules to December 2027 in Digital Omnibus Competitiveness Deal
article.summary: EU co-legislators reached a provisional May 2026 agreement delaying high-risk AI Act obligations to December 2027 and carving out machinery simplifications under the Digital Omnibus package.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

EU lawmakers and member states struck a provisional political agreement on May 7, 2026, to delay key high-risk obligations under the EU Artificial Intelligence Act until December 2027 while introducing targeted simplifications for industrial sectors, as part of the European Commission's Digital Omnibus package aimed at reducing regulatory burdens and enhancing competitiveness [Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/world/eu-countries-lawmakers-strike-provisional-deal-watered-down-ai-rules-2026-05-07/).

The deal, reached in trilogue negotiations between the Council, European Parliament, and Commission, still requires formal endorsement before entering into force [Council of the European Union](https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2026/05/07/artificial-intelligence-council-and-parliament-agree-to-simplify-and-streamline-rules/).

## What We Know

- **High-risk AI timelines pushed back**: Stand-alone high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III — covering uses in biometrics, critical infrastructure, education, employment, law enforcement, border management, and justice — will now see their obligations apply from 2 December 2027 instead of the original 2 August 2026 target. High-risk systems embedded in products subject to other sectoral safety legislation will apply from 2 August 2028 [Council of the European Union](https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2026/05/07/artificial-intelligence-council-and-parliament-agree-to-simplify-and-streamline-rules/).

- **Machinery and industrial simplifications**: AI systems used in machinery, toys, watercraft, and similar sectors receive significant carve-outs. Where machinery is already covered by existing sectoral legislation such as the Machinery Regulation, standalone AI Act obligations are largely removed and shifted to delegated or secondary legislation. This responds to calls from industry players including Siemens and ASML [Euractiv](https://www.euractiv.com/news/eu-clinches-ai-reform-deal-centring-on-machinery-cuts/).

- **New prohibition on non-consensual intimate imagery tools**: The package includes a ban on AI systems intended or likely to generate or manipulate non-consensual intimate or sexual images, video, or audio of identifiable persons, including so-called "nudifier" apps. The prohibition also covers child sexual abuse material and takes effect around December 2026 [Euractiv](https://www.euractiv.com/news/eu-agrees-ban-on-nudifier-ais-and-cuts-rules-for-ai-in-machinery/).

- **Transparency obligations adjusted**: Mandatory watermarking and labelling requirements for AI-generated content are delayed until 2 December 2026, with a shortened grace period in the final text [Council of the European Union](https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2026/05/07/artificial-intelligence-council-and-parliament-agree-to-simplify-and-streamline-rules/).

- **Broader context and drivers**: The changes form part of the Commission's 2025 Digital Omnibus simplification initiative, intended to address delays in harmonised standards and compliance tools, cut administrative costs, and help European businesses compete globally amid concerns over regulatory burden relative to the US and Asia [Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/world/eu-countries-lawmakers-strike-provisional-deal-watered-down-ai-rules-2026-05-07/).

## What We Don't Know

- The exact final legal text and any additional guidance or implementing acts for sectoral overlaps (such as medical devices) remain to be published following legal-linguistic revision.

- Formal adoption timelines by the Council and Parliament, and the precise date the amending regulation will be published in the Official Journal, are still pending confirmation, though targeted before the original August 2026 deadline.

- How national authorities and the AI Office will coordinate enforcement under the clarified competences, and the practical impact on companies already preparing for the original 2026 deadlines, will become clearer only after implementation begins.

## Analysis

The provisional deal represents a pragmatic compromise between the EU's ambitious risk-based regulatory framework and urgent competitiveness pressures. By fixing concrete later dates rather than tying obligations purely to the availability of standards, negotiators have provided greater legal certainty while responding to industry feedback on overlapping rules in machinery and other sectors.

Critics, including consumer organisations and some parliamentarians, have voiced concerns that the simplifications and delays amount to caving to Big Tech and industry lobbying, potentially undermining the Act's original protections for fundamental rights and safety. Supporters, including negotiators from the centre-right EPP group and some member states, argue the changes strike a necessary balance that maintains core safeguards — such as the new nudifier ban — while avoiding unnecessary red tape that could hinder European innovation [Euractiv](https://www.euractiv.com/news/eu-clinches-ai-reform-deal-centring-on-machinery-cuts/).

As one of the world's most comprehensive AI regulatory regimes, the AI Act's evolution will continue to influence global standards. The coming months of formal adoption and guidance development will determine whether the simplifications successfully accelerate responsible AI deployment in Europe without eroding the framework's protective intent. Industry groups have generally welcomed the lighter approach, while calls persist for swift publication of supporting standards and the AI Act Service Desk resources to aid compliance.
